fr:mainmatter.fr:p: A category with zero morphisms is defined to be a category enriched in the category of pointed sets and strict maps. The distinguished point of \mathbf {hom}_{\mathcal {A}}{\mathopen {}\left (A,B\right )\mathclose {}} shall be written 0.

Let \mathcal {A} be a category with zero morphisms. Then the kernel of a morphism A\xrightarrow {f} B in \mathcal {A} is defined to be a morphism K\xrightarrow {k}{A} such that for any X\xrightarrow {h}A with h;f=0, there exists a unique map X\xrightarrow {}K factoring X\xrightarrow {h}A through K\xrightarrow {k}{A} in the sense of the diagram below:
We will often write \operatorname {ker}{f}\xrightarrow {i_{\operatorname {ker}{f}}}A for the kernel of a map A\xrightarrow {f}B in a category with zero morphisms \mathcal {A}.
Evidently, the kernel of a morphism A\xrightarrow {f} B in a category with zero morphisms is the equalizer of A\xrightarrow {f} B with the zero morphism A\xrightarrow {0} B.
Let {\mathopen {}\left (V,I,\otimes ,\alpha ,\lambda ,\rho \right )\mathclose {}} be a monoidal category. A category C enriched in V is defined by the following structure and axioms:

fr:ol.fr:li: for any two objects x,y, an object \mathbf {hom}_{C}{\mathopen {}\left (x,y\right )\mathclose {}}\in V;
for every object x, a morphism I\xrightarrow {i_{x}}\mathbf {hom}_{C}{\mathopen {}\left (x,x\right )\mathclose {}} in V;

for all objects x,y,z, a morphism \mathbf {hom}_{C}{\mathopen {}\left (x,y\right )\mathclose {}}\otimes \mathbf {hom}_{C}{\mathopen {}\left (y,z\right )\mathclose {}}\xrightarrow {c_{x,y,z}}\mathbf {hom}_{C}{\mathopen {}\left (x,z\right )\mathclose {}} in V.
